questions.text: I automate or systematize recurring tasks to reduce the chance of human error.
I prefer it when people say exactly what they mean.
I find that I am generally unaware of the emotional undercurrents in my relationships.
I find that anger in me is a slow-building awareness rather than a flash of heat.
I believe that the felt weight of suffering reveals moral truths that logical analysis alone would miss.
I tend to save the best resources—time, energy, money—for my own priorities.
I notice when an email is bcc'd to someone based on the overly formal tone.
I can look back on difficult periods without re-experiencing the emotional intensity.
I feel energized when a group reaches a genuine consensus.
I believe that processing emotionally charged situations through a framework of analysis rather than feeling is a mature and effective approach.
I believe I process stimuli at a deeper level than most, which is why everything feels so vivid.
I find that I am most motivated when I am working toward something that benefits me directly.
I routinely put community obligations above personal recreation.
I hold the view that remaining objective during emotionally intense experiences is a capacity that improves with practice and is worth cultivating.
I prefer to ask for forgiveness rather than permission.
I prefer to keep my options open rather than lock myself into a specific course of action.
I prefer to find a decision everyone can accept, even if it means compromise.
I find that improvisation gives me more confidence than any methodology in uncertain situations.
